title: Reading practice
subtitle: Stress rules and reading real words

hook.text: You know all 33 letters now. But reading Russian isn't just about knowing letters — it's about knowing which syllable to stress. Stress changes how vowels sound, and getting it wrong can even change the meaning of a word. This lesson gives you the tools to read any Russian word confidently.

why.text: Russian stress is unpredictable — there's no accent mark in normal text to tell you where it falls. But there are patterns, and once you learn the main vowel reduction rules, you'll sound natural even before you have a large vocabulary. Stress is the single biggest factor in sounding 'right' versus sounding like you're reading a textbook.

cards.title: The stress rule
cards.body: Every Russian word with more than one syllable has exactly one stressed syllable. The stressed vowel is pronounced clearly and fully. All other vowels are 'reduced' — they become shorter and shift in sound. There is no fixed rule for which syllable gets the stress; you learn it word by word. Dictionaries mark stress with an accent: молоко́.

examples.meaning: milk — stress on the last syllable
city — stress on the first syllable
good — stress on the last syllable

cards.title: Vowel reduction: О and А
cards.body: When О or А are not stressed, they both reduce to a short 'ah' sound (like the 'a' in 'about'). This is the most important reduction rule in Russian. It means that молоко sounds like 'ma-la-KO' — only the last О gets its full 'o' sound.

examples.meaning: The first two О's reduce to 'ah'
head — both О's reduce, А is stressed

cards.title: Vowel reduction: Е and Я
cards.body: When Е or Я are not stressed, they reduce to a short 'ih' sound (like the 'i' in 'bit'). This is less dramatic than the О/А reduction but still important for natural pronunciation.

examples.meaning: language — the Я is unstressed, sounds like 'yi'

questions.prompt: In молоко, which syllable is stressed?
questions.explanation: молоко́ — stress falls on the last syllable. The first two О's reduce to 'ah'.

questions.prompt: What sound does an unstressed О make?
questions.explanation: Unstressed О reduces to a short 'ah' sound, like the 'a' in 'about'.

questions.prompt: How would you approximately pronounce город?
questions.explanation: го́род — stress on the first syllable. The unstressed О in the second syllable reduces to 'ah', giving GO-rad.

cards.title: Stress can change meaning
cards.body: In some Russian words, moving the stress changes the meaning entirely. This is rare at the beginner level, but it's good to be aware of. The most famous example: за́мок (castle) vs замо́к (lock).

cards.title: Reading strategy
cards.body: When you encounter a new word: (1) identify all the vowels, (2) if you know the stress, reduce the unstressed vowels, (3) if you don't know the stress, try stressing different syllables — one will sound more familiar. Over time, you'll build intuition for common stress patterns.

examples.meaning: thank you — stress on the second syllable
word — stress on the first syllable

questions.prompt: Which syllable is stressed in спасибо?
questions.explanation: спаси́бо — stress is on the second syllable 'си'.
молоко́ = milk. Remember: ma-la-KO with stress on the last syllable.
Unstressed О always reduces to a short 'ah' sound.

questions.prompt: How do you pronounce the О in Москва?
questions.explanation: Москва́ — stress is on the last syllable (ва), so the О is unstressed and reduces to 'ah': ma-SKVA.

rules: Every multi-syllable Russian word has one stressed syllable — learn it with the word
Unstressed О and А both reduce to a short 'ah' sound
Unstressed Е and Я reduce to a short 'ih' sound
Stress can change meaning: за́мок (castle) vs замо́к (lock)
Reading strategy: find the vowels, apply stress, reduce the rest
summary.nextLesson: Next: Greetings — hello, goodbye, and the polite basics for real conversations.

What sound does an unstressed А make?
In голова, what happens to the two О's?
options: They reduce to 'ah' because they are unstressed
questions.answer: They reduce to 'ah' because they are unstressed

questions.prompt: за́мок means 'castle'. What does замо́к mean?
